title: Files, paths and JSON

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial chapters 7-8: input/output, files, JSON, exceptions, and cleanup; atomic topic: Files, paths and JSON
sources.claim: Python file persistence uses pathlib paths, explicit text encoding, context-managed I/O, and schema-aware JSON validation with documented handling for missing, malformed, partial, or forbidden data.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Python file persistence uses pathlib paths, explicit text encoding, context-managed I/O, and schema-aware JSON validation with documented handling for missing, malformed, partial, or forbidden data.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A loader reads UTF-8 through Path.open, catches missing and decode failures separately, and validates required keys and value types before constructing domain data. Your goal is to implement file persistence that handles missing, malformed and permission-failure cases. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Function contracts and returns; Unicode text and formatting; Exception handling and propagation; Dictionaries and sets. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python file persistence uses pathlib paths, explicit text encoding, context-managed I/O, and schema-aware JSON validation with documented handling for missing, malformed, partial, or forbidden data. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Relative paths use the process working directory, JSON types do not enforce a domain schema, writes can be partial, ensure_ascii is a representation option, and untrusted sizes or nesting need bounds. The learner must reject this documented misconception: json.load validates application fields and open automatically chooses UTF-8 and the script directory.

includes: Read and write text with explicit encoding and validate structured JSON data.
Implement file persistence that handles missing, malformed and permission-failure cases.
excludes: Relative paths use the process working directory, JSON types do not enforce a domain schema, writes can be partial, ensure_ascii is a representation option, and untrusted sizes or nesting need bounds.

outcomes: Implement file persistence that handles missing, malformed and permission-failure cases.

examples.title: Files, paths and JSON: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A save writes a temporary sibling then replaces the target under a documented policy rather than assuming one text write is crash-atomic.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A save writes a temporary sibling then replaces the target under a documented policy rather than assuming one text write is crash-atomic.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Relative paths use the process working directory, JSON types do not enforce a domain schema, writes can be partial, ensure_ascii is a representation option, and untrusted sizes or nesting need bounds.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: json.load validates application fields and open automatically chooses UTF-8 and the script directory.

analogy.example: a records office that escalates named failures, validates decoded forms, and returns borrowed resources on every exit can introduce Files, paths and JSON, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
